intro: Conversion rate is the percentage of visitors who complete a desired action — a purchase, signup, form submission, or download. It is the most direct measure of how well a website or landing page is working. Enter your visitor count, conversions, and optional revenue per conversion to see your conversion rate, total revenue generated, and revenue per visitor — key metrics for any digital business.
howItWorks: Conversion rate = (conversions ÷ visitors) × 100. Total revenue = conversions × revenue per conversion. Revenue per visitor (RPV) = total revenue ÷ visitors. For example, 5,000 visitors with 75 conversions = 1.5% conversion rate. At $49 revenue per conversion, total revenue = $3,675, RPV = $0.74 per visitor. RPV is particularly useful for paid traffic decisions: if you can acquire a visitor for $0.50 and each visitor generates $0.74, the traffic is profitable.
interpretationGuide: Conversion rates vary widely by industry, traffic source, and conversion goal. eCommerce purchase conversion: 1–3% is typical, 3–5% is good, 5%+ is excellent. Lead generation (form submission): 5–15% typical. Landing pages for high-value offers: 2–5% typical. Free trial signups: 5–15%. Email opt-ins: 10–40%. Traffic source also matters — direct and email traffic converts higher than paid social; organic search typically converts better than display ads. Always interpret your rate in the context of your specific conversion goal and traffic source.

faqs.question: What is a good conversion rate for an eCommerce website?
faqs.answer: eCommerce conversion rates (completed purchases ÷ sessions): Industry average: 1–3%. Good: 3–5%. Excellent: 5%+. The top quartile of eCommerce sites converts at 3.7%+ according to industry benchmarks. Rates vary significantly by traffic source: email traffic often converts at 4–7%; organic search at 2–3%; paid social at 0.5–2%; direct traffic at 3–5%. Category matters too — high-consideration products (furniture, electronics) typically convert at lower rates than impulse buys.

faqs.question: What is CRO (conversion rate optimization)?
faqs.answer: CRO is the systematic process of increasing the percentage of visitors who take a desired action. It involves: identifying friction points in the user journey through analytics and heatmaps; forming hypotheses about what would improve conversions; running A/B tests to validate those hypotheses; and implementing winning changes. Common CRO tactics: clearer CTAs, faster page load, simplified checkout, social proof (reviews, trust badges), reducing form fields, and improving mobile experience.

faqs.question: What is the difference between conversion rate and click-through rate?
faqs.answer: Click-through rate (CTR) measures the percentage of people who click a link or ad out of those who saw it: CTR = clicks ÷ impressions × 100. Conversion rate measures the percentage of visitors (who arrived at your site) who completed a goal: CVR = conversions ÷ visitors × 100. CTR happens before a visitor arrives; conversion rate happens after. Both are important: high CTR with low CVR means the ad is appealing but the landing page is disappointing. Low CTR with high CVR means the ad is underselling a strong product.

faqs.question: How do I track conversions on my website?
faqs.answer: Google Analytics 4 (GA4) is the primary free tool: set up events for purchase, form submission, sign-up, or any goal action; configure GA4 goals/conversions based on those events. For eCommerce, implement enhanced ecommerce tracking to capture transaction revenue. Meta Pixel tracks conversions from Facebook/Instagram ads. Google Ads conversion tracking connects ad spend to conversions. Server-side conversion APIs (Facebook CAPI, Google Enhanced Conversions) improve accuracy by reducing tracking loss from ad blockers and iOS privacy changes.

faqs.question: What is revenue per visitor (RPV)?
faqs.answer: RPV = total revenue ÷ total visitors. It is the average revenue generated by each visitor, regardless of whether they convert. RPV is the key metric for evaluating paid traffic profitability: if a visitor costs $0.40 to acquire (cost per click) and generates $0.35 RPV, the traffic is unprofitable. RPV combines conversion rate and average order value into one number and is particularly useful for comparing traffic sources: source A at 2% CVR and $50 AOV = $1.00 RPV; source B at 4% CVR and $25 AOV = $1.00 RPV — identical profitability.

faqs.question: What is a micro-conversion?
faqs.answer: A micro-conversion is a small action that indicates progress toward the main conversion goal: email signup, adding a product to cart, watching a product video, creating an account, or clicking a pricing page. Tracking micro-conversions reveals where users drop off in the funnel and what intermediate steps correlate with eventual purchase. Someone who adds to cart is 5–10x more likely to purchase than someone who only viewed a product page, so cart abandonment recovery is high-ROI.

faqs.question: Why is my conversion rate dropping?
faqs.answer: Common causes of declining conversion rate: Traffic quality change (new traffic source with lower purchase intent), seasonal variation, a recent site update that broke UX or checkout, slower page load speed, mobile experience issues (check mobile vs. desktop CVR separately), pricing changes, competitor pressure, or changes in ad targeting that attract less qualified visitors. Segment your conversion rate by traffic source, device, and landing page to identify where the drop is occurring.

faqs.question: What is a good conversion rate for a landing page?
faqs.answer: Landing page conversion rates vary by offer type: Lead generation (free guide, webinar): 10–25%. Free trial signup: 5–15%. Demo request: 3–8%. Product purchase (cold traffic): 1–3%. Email opt-in (pop-up): 5–15%. Rates from warm audiences (retargeting, email) are 3–5x higher than cold traffic. WordStream reports average landing page conversion at 2.35%, with the top 25% of pages converting at 5.31%+ and the top 10% at 11.45%+.

faqs.question: How does A/B testing improve conversion rates?
faqs.answer: A/B testing compares two versions of a page (or element) to see which converts better. Version A gets 50% of traffic; Version B gets 50%. Run until statistical significance is reached (typically 95% confidence with enough conversions in each variant — usually 100+ conversions per variant minimum). Test one element at a time: headline, hero image, CTA button color/text, form length, pricing display, social proof. Use tools like Google Optimize (sunset), VWO, Optimizely, or Unbounce for structured testing.

faqs.question: What affects eCommerce conversion rate most?
faqs.answer: Top factors by impact: page load speed (each 1-second delay reduces conversions by 7%); mobile experience (70%+ of traffic is mobile but mobile CVR lags desktop by 2–3x on most sites); checkout friction (forced account creation alone can cost 35% of checkouts); product images and descriptions (multiple high-quality images, size guides, videos); trust signals (reviews, security badges, return policy clearly displayed); and cart abandonment recovery (email + retargeting ad sequences).

faqs.question: What is the average eCommerce conversion rate by industry?
faqs.answer: eCommerce conversion rates by vertical (approximate, varies by source): Arts and crafts: 3.4%. Pets: 2.5%. Food and beverage: 3.6%. Health and beauty: 2.7%. Fashion and apparel: 1.5–2.5%. Electronics: 1.0–1.5%. Furniture/home: 0.5–1.5%. Jewelry: 1.0–2.0%. Sporting goods: 1.5–2.5%. Consumer electronics tend to have lower CVR because purchases require more research; food and impulse categories convert higher. These figures are directional — your segment, brand strength, and traffic source will differ.

faqs.question: Should I optimize conversion rate or drive more traffic?
faqs.answer: CRO before more traffic. Doubling conversion rate doubles revenue without increasing ad spend — it is effectively free. Doubling traffic doubles revenue but also doubles cost. Example: 10,000 visitors/month, 1% CVR, $50 AOV = $5,000 revenue. Improving CVR to 2% = $10,000 (no extra cost). Doubling traffic = $10,000 but costs an additional $X in ad spend. Do both, but fix your conversion rate first so every marginal visitor is generating its full potential value.
